metric_name: First programming job at Wang subsidiary
metric_value: In June 1969 I was a few months into my first programming job at a subsidiary of Wang Laboratories, combining a handful of labor accounting programs written in COBOL.

metric_name: IBM unbundling of software and services
metric_value: On June 23, 1969, IBM — then the most powerful force in computing and under antitrust fire — unbundled software and services from its mostly-mainframe business model, catalyzing the modern independent software industry.
Wang would later become a big player in the 1980s; desktop computers were still being invented by some colleagues a few offices away in 1969.

My computer was an IBM 360-65, top of the line mainframe ancestor of today's IBM Z-series. Had tapes, disks, telecom, printers. About 1/400th of the computing power of an iPhone 3G — cost over $10 million.
When you bought or rented an IBM 360, it came bundled with IBM systems engineers to care for IBM software and typically the customer's software too.

In 1969 there were few choices in software development: few small custom-programming shops, most software developed in-house very slowly and at great cost. Programmers uncommon; no modeling tools; systems analysis unsystematized; computer time very expensive.
John Cullinane built Culprit, a report generator, then IDMS network database — among the earliest independent software products.
Charles Wang founded Computer Associates around a mainframe tape library utility — showing anyone could build an independent software business.
Gene Amdahl, IBM 360 architect, founded Amdahl Corp. and made plug-compatible mainframes at lower cost than IBM.
The 1970s spawned the independent minicomputer industry: DEC, Data General, Wang, Prime Computer, and eventually Sun Microsystems — all independent, none beholden to IBM.
In the 1980s IBM picked Microsoft and Intel for its new PC business — further validating independent software/hardware ecosystem model.
